g.data-narration: The jakarta.servlet.http package provides HTTP-specific implementations for building robust server-side web applications in Java.
g.text: • 98% of web interactions use HTTP protocol (2023 Web Almanac)
• Built-in session management via cookies & URL rewriting

g.data-narration: Five key classes form the foundation: HttpServlet for request handling, HttpServletRequest/Response for data, HttpSession for state, and Cookie for persistence.
HttpServlet is the abstract base class for HTTP servlets. It routes requests to appropriate doGet(), doPost(), doPut(), and doDelete() methods.
HttpServletRequest encapsulates all HTTP request data including parameters, headers, cookies, and session information from the client.
HttpServletResponse manages the HTTP response including setting content type, status codes, headers, cookies, and redirecting the client.
g.text: Status Codes: 200 OK | 302 Found | 404 Not Found | 500 Server Error

g.data-narration: HttpSession provides server-side session tracking, maintaining user state across multiple HTTP requests using session IDs stored in cookies.
Cookie stores client-side data persistently. Created with name/value pair, configured with maxAge, then added to response for sending to client.
HTTP methods map to servlet methods: GET retrieves data, POST submits form data, PUT updates resources, DELETE removes resources.
The flow: Client sends HTTP request → Container creates request/response objects → service() routes to doXxx() → Servlet processes → Response returned.
g.text: Container creates HttpServletRequest → service() routes to doXxx() → Servlet processes → Response returned

g.data-narration: Example: A welcome servlet handles GET requests, extracts the 'user' parameter, and returns an HTML greeting to the client.
g.text: protected void doGet(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse res) {...}

g.data-narration: This servlet tracks visits by reading cookies from the request, incrementing the count, and sending back an updated cookie to the client.
This shopping cart example uses HttpSession to store cart items server-side, maintaining state across multiple requests from the same client.
Key takeaways: HttpServlet handles HTTP methods, Request provides parameter/header access, Response controls output, Session maintains server state, Cookie stores client data.
Remember: sendRedirect() vs forward(), getSession(true) vs getSession(false), always set content type, and session is server-side while cookies are client-side.
g.text: • Adding cookies to request instead of response
sendRedirect() → New request | forward() → Same request
Session: Server-side secure | Cookie: Client-side 4KB limit

g.data-narration: Real-world applications: E-commerce uses sessions for carts, banking uses secure sessions, social media uses cookies for auth, and APIs use HTTP methods for REST services.
